import java.util.Arrays;

public class Solution {
    public boolean canBeEqual(int[] target, int[] arr) {
        int[] targetCopy = Arrays.copyOf(target, target.length);
        int[] arrCopy = Arrays.copyOf(arr, arr.length);
        Arrays.sort(targetCopy);
        Arrays.sort(arrCopy);
        return Arrays.equals(targetCopy, arrCopy);
    }
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canBeEqual(int[] target, int[] arr) {
        int[] targetCopy = java.util.Arrays.copyOf(target, target.length);
        int[] arrCopy = java.util.Arrays.copyOf(arr, arr.length);
        java.util.Arrays.sort(targetCopy);
        java.util.Arrays.sort(arrCopy);
        return java.util.Arrays.equals(targetCopy, arrCopy);
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n log n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The approach checks if the sorted versions of the target and arr arrays are identical. This works because any permutation achievable via subarray reversals must have the same multiset of elements. Strengths include simplicity and optimal time complexity. Weaknesses involve O(n) space for array copies.
